schools.summary: Gill: Paul was a prisoner not actively (whom Christ had apprehended by his grace) but passively, made a prisoner for Christ, on account of preaching Christ and his gospel — not for any capital crime, as theft or murder; therefore he was not ashamed of his bonds but rather gloried in them, a prison often being the very port of the gospel's advance; Paul, beginning a prayer (resumed at v.14), describing himself as the prisoner of Jesus Christ for the sake of the Gentiles — the honoured bondage borne for the Gentiles (Paul, far from ashamed of his imprisonment, naming himself 'the prisoner of Jesus Christ,' counting his chains an honour, for he was bound not as a criminal but for the very cause of bringing the gospel to the Gentiles, his bonds the badge of his faithful service).

themes: I Paul, the prisoner of Jesus Christ
for you Gentiles
the honoured bondage borne for the Gentiles
his chains the badge of faithful service

schools.summary: Gill: the 'dispensation of the grace of God' is not the free love and favour of God in his heart, nor internal grace wrought in the apostle, but either the gift of grace qualifying him for the ministry, or rather the doctrine of grace, the gospel, the subject matter of his ministry, committed to him as a trust; Paul referring to the dispensation of the grace of God given him toward the Gentiles, which they had heard of — the stewardship of the gospel for the Gentiles (Paul speaking of the special trust, the stewardship, of God's grace committed to him: the office and commission to dispense the gospel of grace to the Gentiles, of which they had heard, so his apostleship to them was no self-chosen role but a divine stewardship of grace laid upon him for their good).

themes: the dispensation of the grace of God
which is given me to you-ward
the stewardship of the gospel for the Gentiles
a divine trust laid on Paul

schools.summary: Gill: the gospel is sometimes called a mystery — the mystery of the gospel, of godliness, of faith — its several doctrines being the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven (a trinity of persons in the Godhead and the like); Paul declaring that by revelation God made known to him the mystery, as he had briefly written before — the mystery received by divine revelation (Paul receiving the gospel-mystery, chiefly the truth of the Gentiles' inclusion in Christ, not from men nor by study but by direct revelation from God, who unveiled to him what had been hidden, so his message rested on God's own disclosure, not human invention).

themes: by revelation he made known unto me the mystery
as I wrote afore in few words
the mystery received by divine revelation
not from men nor by study, but from God

schools.summary: Gill: by reading the foregoing chapters and seriously considering what is contained in them, they may perceive Paul's knowledge; Barnes: by the bare reading of which you may understand the view he entertains of the plan of salvation, particularly God's intention in regard to the salvation of the Gentiles; Paul observing that, when they read what he has written, they may understand his knowledge in the mystery of Christ — the apostle's insight open to the reader (Paul, far from withholding his understanding, setting down in writing such things that any who read with care will perceive the depth of his insight into 'the mystery of Christ,' the whole gospel-secret of redemption and the Gentiles' calling, so his readers are made sharers in the revelation given him).

themes: when ye read, ye may understand
my knowledge in the mystery of Christ
the apostle's insight open to the reader
readers made sharers in the revelation

schools.summary: Gill: the mystery of Christ and the gospel was not made known to men in general, nor so clearly, under former ages — though some hints were given to Adam immediately after his fall, the gospel preached before to Abraham, and Moses and David and others knew something of it — but it is now more fully dispensed; Paul declaring that the mystery, not made known in former ages as it now is, has been revealed to the holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit — the new clearness of the gospel revelation (the truth now made plain, the Gentiles' full and equal sharing in Christ, once hidden or only dimly intimated, now clearly unveiled by the Spirit to the New Testament apostles and prophets, the bearers of the completed revelation, so what the saints of old saw afar off is now openly displayed).

themes: which in other ages was not made known unto the sons of men
now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets
by the Spirit
the new clearness of the gospel revelation

schools.summary: Gill: the Gentiles should be fellowheirs with the Jews of all the blessings of grace, of lasting salvation, and of the eternal, incorruptible, never-fading inheritance in heaven; heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ, inheriting all things — heirs by being Christ's, by their adoption through him, and their justification by his righteousness; Paul stating the very content of the mystery — that the Gentiles should be fellowheirs, of the same body, and partakers of God's promise in Christ through the gospel — the full equality of the Gentiles in Christ (the great secret now revealed being the Gentiles' complete and equal share with the Jews in the privileges of grace: joint heirs of the same inheritance, members of the same body the church, and partakers of the same promise, all in Christ and through the gospel, the old wall between Jew and Gentile wholly abolished).

themes: the Gentiles should be fellowheirs, and of the same body
partakers of his promise in Christ by the gospel
the substance of the mystery
the Gentiles' full equality in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was made a minister of the gospel not by men but by God; he is a true minister of the gospel who is called of God to the work of the ministry, qualified by him with grace and gifts for it, and who faithfully discharges it according to the ability God has given; Paul declaring that he was made a minister of this gospel according to the gift of God's grace, given by the effectual working of his power — the ministry as a gift of grace and power (Paul ascribing his whole ministry to God: he became a minister not by his own choosing or worth but by the free gift of God's grace, and was made fit and effective for it by the mighty, effectual working of God's power, so both his office and his ability to fill it were wholly of God).

themes: whereof I was made a minister
according to the gift of the grace of God
by the effectual working of his power
office and ability both wholly of God

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the surpassing lowliness of Paul, who, when about to speak of the vastness of God's grace, calls himself 'less than the least of all saints' — coining a word beyond grammar (a comparative on a superlative) to express his deep sense of his own unworthiness; Paul declaring that to him, less than the least of all saints, this grace was given, to preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ — the unworthy preacher of inexhaustible riches (Paul, with the deepest self-abasement, counting himself below the lowest of the saints, remembering his former persecutions, marvelling that so high a grace was given to one so unworthy: the commission to proclaim to the Gentiles the 'unsearchable riches of Christ,' treasures of grace and glory so vast they can never be traced out or exhausted).

themes: unto me, who am less than the least of all saints
is this grace given
preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ
deep humility joined to high commission

schools.summary: Gill: the 'fellowship of the mystery' (or the dispensation of it) is the communication of grace by the gospel, a participation of its truths and doctrines, and the communion with Father, Son, and Spirit to which the gospel calls and leads; Paul declaring his charge to make all men see the fellowship of the mystery, which from the world's beginning had been hidden in God, the Creator of all things by Jesus Christ — the open publication of what was hidden in God (Paul's task to bring into the light, for all to see, the gospel-mystery that had lain concealed in God's own eternal counsel from the foundation of the world, now at last unveiled, the same God who had hidden it being the Creator of all things by Christ).

themes: to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery
which from the beginning of the world hath been hid in God
who created all things by Jesus Christ
the open publication of the hidden mystery

schools.summary: Gill: by 'principalities and powers in heavenly places' are meant not civil magistrates, much less evil angels, but the good angels, the angels in heaven; Paul declaring the further purpose — that now, through the church, the manifold wisdom of God might be made known to the principalities and powers in heavenly places — the church as the theatre of God's wisdom to angels (God's grand design in the gospel-mystery reaching even to the angelic world: the holy angels, beholding the church, Jew and Gentile gathered into one body by grace, learning and admiring the 'manifold,' many-coloured and richly varied, wisdom of God, which devised so wonderful a salvation, the redeemed company being the very display before heaven of God's wisdom).

debates.question: Who are the 'principalities and powers' that learn God's wisdom by the church?

sides.position: The good angels in heaven — who, beholding the church (Jew and Gentile in one body), learn and admire God's manifold wisdom

themes: unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places
might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God
the church the theatre of God's wisdom to angels
the manifold, many-coloured wisdom of God

schools.summary: Gill: the whole of salvation, in which is displayed the great wisdom of God, is according to a purpose of his — the scheme fixed in the council of peace, the thing itself effected in pursuance of it, Christ the Redeemer set forth in it, his incarnation, the time of his coming, his sufferings and death with all their circumstances decreed by God; Paul declaring that all this is according to the eternal purpose which God purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord — the grounding of the whole plan in God's eternal counsel (the entire mystery, the Gentiles' calling, the church, the display of wisdom to angels, no afterthought or accident of history but the unfolding in time of a purpose God had formed in eternity and centred in Christ, so the gospel rests on the firm foundation of God's everlasting design).

themes: according to the eternal purpose
which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord
the whole plan grounded in God's eternal counsel
no afterthought, but purposed in eternity

schools.summary: Gill: in Christ we have access into the holy of holies, to the throne of grace there, and to God the Father as seated on it — Christ being the way of access, union to him giving the right of access, his mediation procuring audience of God and acceptance, both of person and service; and this access is with boldness, denoting liberty of coming granted by God and a liberty in their own souls to speak out their minds plainly; Paul declaring that in Christ believers have boldness and access to God with confidence through faith in him — the free and confident approach to God through Christ (believers drawing near not timidly or as strangers but with boldness, freedom of speech, access, an introduction into the very presence of the Father, and confidence, settled assurance, all received by faith in Christ, whose mediation opens the way).

themes: in whom we have boldness and access
with confidence
by the faith of him
the free and confident approach to God through Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was a man attended with many tribulations and great afflictions, which he suffered not as an evildoer (from God or men); he was not ashamed of them but gloried and took pleasure in them, having much of God's presence in them, never taken unawares, always expecting them and helped to look to the glory that should follow; Paul desiring that they not faint at his tribulations endured for them, which are their glory — the sufferings of the apostle turned to the church's honour (Paul, knowing his imprisonment might dishearten the Gentile believers, as if their cause were shameful or failing, entreating them not to lose heart, for his very afflictions, borne for their sakes in defending and advancing their gospel, are not their shame but their glory, a token of how precious their salvation is).

themes: that ye faint not at my tribulations for you
which is your glory
the apostle's sufferings turned to the church's honour
a token of how precious their salvation is

schools.summary: Gill: Paul prays for the perseverance of the saints, which is the pure gift of God and what he has promised, and therefore to be sought for (for what God has designed and promised, he will be sought to), his view also being to stir up the saints to pray for themselves; the gesture he uses, bowing the knees, expresses reverence and humility in prayer; Paul declaring that for this cause he bows his knees to the Father of the Lord Jesus Christ — the reverent posture of earnest intercession (Paul, resuming the prayer begun in v.1, turning to fervent intercession for the Ephesians, and marking its earnestness and reverence by the very posture of his body: bowing his knees, kneeling, before the Father, the God and Father of the Lord Jesus, in humble, worshipful supplication for their spiritual establishment).

themes: for this cause I bow my knees
unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
the reverent posture of earnest intercession
resuming the great prayer for the Ephesians

schools.summary: Gill: this may refer either to God the Father of Christ, who is the Father of the whole family in heaven and in earth (not only the Father of Christ but the Father of spirits, of angelic spirits and of the souls of men, and the Father of all the saints by adopting grace, whether above or below, all the household of God), or to Christ the everlasting Father; Paul observing that of the Father the whole family in heaven and earth is named — the one family of God in heaven and earth (Paul, having named the Father, marking that from him the entire family of God takes its name and being: the holy angels and the glorified saints above, and the believers still on earth below, all one household, deriving their sonship and their very name as God's family from the one Father, so heaven and earth are united in him).

themes: of whom the whole family
in heaven and earth is named
the one family of God in heaven and earth
all deriving their name from the one Father

schools.summary: Gill: the grant is according to, and out of, that rich, plenteous, and glorious fulness of grace and strength in Christ Jesus; Barnes: according to the glorious abundance of his mercy (Philippians 4:19), out of those stores of rich grace that can never be exhausted, the word 'riches' denoting abundance; Paul praying that God would grant them, according to the riches of his glory, to be strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man — the inward strengthening from God's rich glory (Paul's first great request being for inward power: that God, out of the boundless riches of his glory, not sparingly but in proportion to his own infinite fulness, would strengthen the believers with might by his Spirit in the 'inner man,' the renewed soul, the hidden person of the heart, making them mighty within for faith, holiness, and endurance).

themes: according to the riches of his glory
to be strengthened with might by his Spirit
in the inner man
inward power from God's rich glory

schools.summary: Gill: the inhabitant Christ is he who dwells in the highest heavens, who dwells in the Father and the Father in him, in whom all fulness dwells, the fulness of the Godhead and the fulness of grace, so that those in whose hearts he dwells cannot want any good thing and are in the greatest safety; Paul praying that Christ may dwell in their hearts by faith, they being rooted and grounded in love — the indwelling Christ and the foundation of love (Paul's prayer rising to its heart: that Christ himself may take up a settled, permanent dwelling in their hearts, received and retained by faith, and that they, like a tree well-rooted and a building well-founded, may be firmly established in love, God's love to them and theirs to him and one another, the soil and foundation from which all spiritual growth and stability spring).

themes: that Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith
being rooted and grounded in love
the indwelling Christ
the foundation of love

schools.summary: Gill: this is the end of their being rooted and grounded in love — that they, together with the rest of the saints interested in it, might have a larger and more comprehensive view; Barnes: it was Paul's desire that others, as well as they, might understand and appreciate the wonders of redemption; Paul praying that they may be able to comprehend, with all saints, the breadth, length, depth, and height — the grasp of the immeasurable love and grace of God (Paul praying the believers, rooted in love, may be enabled, in company with all the saints, to take in the vast dimensions of God's love in Christ, or of the whole mystery of redemption, a greatness so immense it can be apprehended only in part, and only by the whole communion of the saints together).

themes: may be able to comprehend with all saints
what is the breadth, and length, and depth, and height
the immeasurable dimensions of God's love
apprehended only with all the saints

schools.summary: Gill: the love of Christ to his own, his church and people, is special and peculiar, free and sovereign, as early as the Father's love, durable and unchangeable, the greatest love ever heard of, matchless and unparalleled, exceeding strong and affectionate, wonderful and surprising (its instances his engaging as surety, his espousing them, etc.); Paul praying that they might know the love of Christ which passes knowledge, that they might be filled with all the fulness of God — the knowledge of the boundless love and the fulness of God (Paul's prayer reaching its height in two immense petitions: that they may 'know,' by experience and in measure, the love of Christ which yet surpasses all knowledge, so vast it can never be fully comprehended, and that they may be 'filled with all the fulness of God,' filled according to their capacity with the graces and likeness of God, as full of God as a creature can be).

debates.question: How can one 'know the love of Christ which passeth knowledge'?

sides.position: By knowing it truly in experience and in measure, while it yet surpasses full comprehension — knowing in part a love so vast it can never be wholly grasped

themes: to know the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge
that ye might be filled with all the fulness of God
the boundless love of Christ
filled, according to capacity, with God's fulness

schools.summary: Gill: this conclusion of the prayer celebrates the power of God, a perfection essential to him and very large and extensive — reaching to all things, to every thing he wills (his actual or ordinative power), to more than he has willed (his absolute power), to all things past, present, and future, and to things impossible to nature; Paul ascribing praise to God, who is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that works in believers — the God whose power exceeds all asking and thought (Paul, his great prayer ended, breaking into praise of the God whose ability infinitely surpasses even the largest petitions and highest imaginings of his people, piling word on word, 'exceeding abundantly above all,' to reach after the immeasurable, and grounding the assurance of it in the very power already at work within them by the Spirit).

themes: able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think
according to the power that worketh in us
the God whose power exceeds all asking and thought
grounded in the power already at work within

schools.summary: Gill: this doxology, or ascription of glory to God, with which Paul concludes his prayer, gives glory to God on account of his perfections (to be celebrated), the works of creation and providence (to be commended and acquiesced in), temporal mercies (for which thanks should be given), and especially spiritual mercies; glory rendered in the church, the proper place, and by Christ Jesus, the only Mediator; Paul ascribing glory to God in the church by Christ Jesus throughout all ages, world without end, sealed with 'Amen' — the eternal glory of God in the church through Christ (Paul ending the doxology by giving all glory to God and naming the place and the means: glory rendered 'in the church,' the redeemed company being the great theatre and instrument of his praise, and 'by Christ Jesus,' the one Mediator through whom alone acceptable praise ascends, and that without end, through all generations and into eternity).

themes: unto him be glory in the church by Christ Jesus
throughout all ages, world without end. Amen
the eternal glory of God in the church
the church the theatre of praise, Christ the Mediator
